MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Plaintiffs Notice Of And Motion For Summary Judgment Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Oct-31-2019 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Ryan Mau, Esq. 415-296-7700 for Plaintiff.
Andrew Quan, Esq. 510-394-2466 for Defendant.

RULING - A R G U E D. Defendant argues. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

PLAINTIFF JAMEL BOUZIDI, SOFIENE BOUZIDI's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication.

The Cout adopts the tentative ruling. Plaintiffs Jamel Bouzidi and Sofiene Bouzidi's motion for summary judgment is granted. Plaintiffs establish that they are entitled to $177,321.88 in damages based upon defendant's breach of the settlement agreement.

Plaintiffs establish a prima facie case for breach of contract and the court overrules defendant's evidentiary objections. Plaintiffs demonstrate personal knowledge regarding the matters in their declarations and the IRS documents are admissible as official acts. (Evid. Code, 452(c) and 1280.)

The parties entered into a binding settlement agreement and defendant, who was represented by counsel, fails to show that the settlement was the product of economic duress. (Raouf Bouzidi Dec., 23; CrossTalk Productions, Inc. v. Jacobson (1998) 65 Cal.App.4th 631, 644 ["If a reasonable alternative was available, and there hence was no compelling necessity to submit to the coercive demands, economic duress cannot be established."].) Defendant cannot manufacture a triable issue of fact by contradicting his sworn July 21, 2016 declaration, in which he stated, "I have not been induced or threatened in any manner in providing this declaration and do so knowingly, intelligently, freely, and voluntarily." (See D'Amico v. Board of Medical Examiners (1974) 11 Cal.3d 1, 21.) Plaintiffs have performed all material terms of the settlement agreement. (UMF 7-10.) Paragraph 3.2 of the settlement agreement provides that defendant is liable for the plaintiffs' taxes. Defendant has breached the settlement agreement by failing to pay the taxes. (UMF 12.) As the court noted when it overruled defendant's prior demurrer, the contract is not illegal. This is especially true because defendant previously admitted that: "I alone am solely liable and responsible for any and all taxes penalties, fines, interest, costs, fees and attorney's fees regarding the operation of Chez Fayala, LLC." (Mau Dec., Ex. C (Raouf Bouzidi Decl., 5; Evid. Code, 1123 and 1220.) There is no violation of Civil Code, 1668 because there is no evidence that plaintiffs acted illegally and defendant admits that he was responsible for the taxes.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details) Plaintiff to submit a Proposed form of Judgment.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Apr-15-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Ryan Mau, Esq, 415-296-7700, appearing for Plaintiffs;
Michael Epinosa, Esq Law offices of Andrew Quan, 510-394-2466, appearing for Defendant Raouf Bouzidi, et al;

RULING - A R G U E D. Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows: The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows

Defendant Raouf Bouzidi's demurrer to plaintiffs Jamel Bouzidi and Sofiene Bouzidi's complaint is overruled as to the breach of contract cause of action and sustained with leave to amend as to the unjust enrichment cause of action. Plaintiffs have alleged the existence of a valid contract. On its face, the settlement agreement does not have the unlawful object of circumventing federal income tax law; rather, it provides that defendant is "solely liable and responsible for any and all taxes, penalties, fines, interest, costs, fees, and attorney's fees" in relation to the operation of the restaurant business Chez Fayala, LLC, including any tax liability that Plaintiffs shall have for that business beyond their salary or W-2 earnings, and further that he shall indemnify Plantiffs against any claims arising out of the operation of that business. (Settlement Agreement and Release 3.2.1, 3.2.2.). Defendant cites no authority finding unlawful an agreement to assume responsibility for tax liabilities. " 'Whether a contract is illegal . . . is a question of law to be determined from the circumstances of each particular case.'" (Kashani v. Tsann Kuen China Enterprise Co. (2004) 118 Cal.App.4th 531, 540.) Further, " '[a]s a general rule, if a contract can be performed legally, a court will presume that the parties intended a lawful mode of performance.'" (Id. at 549.) The contract is not unlawful on its face under Civ. Code 1667, 2773 and 2774 under the particular facts alleged in this case, where the settlement agreement incorporates a sworn declaration by Defendant admitting that he was solely responsible for all decisions and actions related to all of the operations of Chez Fayala LLC, Plaintiffs did not benefit from his actions, and Defendant has sole responsibility and liability for any taxes, penalties and fines regarding the operation of Chez Fayala, LLC from its inception until its winding down. While the provision of the settlement agreement referring to indemnification against criminal liability for tax fraud or evasion or criminal money laundering is likely unenforceable, the complaint does not allege that the breach of contract arises out of any such facts; it alleges only that upon the conclusion of a federal tax audit, "an amount for federal income taxes owed was determined" which Defendant has refused to pay. (Compl. 15.) Nor has Defendant established that provision cannot be severed. "Where the consideration is only partly illegal and the agreement is severable, the legal portion may be enforced." (1 Witkin, Summary of California Law, Contracts (11th ed. 2018) 422; see also id. 423 [liberal rule of severability].) Although there is a split of authority as to whether unjust enrichment is a cause of action, it is routinely pled as a restitution or quasi contract theory of liability. (Durell v. Sharp Healthcare (2010) 183 Cal.App.4th 1350, 1370.) The elements of a cause of action for unjust enrichment are simply stated as receipt of a benefit and unjust retention of the benefit at the expense of another. (Professional Tax Appeal (2018) 29 Cal.App.5th 230, 238.) The complaint adequately pleads those elements. (Compl. 22-23.) However, in order to assert a claim for restitution based on unjust enrichment, Plaintiffs must make it clear that the claim is an inconsistent claim alleging the absence of an enforceable agreement. (See Klein v. Chevron USA, Inc. (2012) 202 Cal.App.4th 1342, 1389.)

Order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: W. Trupek, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano CSR#8600 email maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
